1: A shadow: YHWH spoke to Moses about the sin offering (chatat). The sin offering chapter introduces a new category: the offering for shegagah (unintentional sin, mistakes, errors). The Levitical system distinguishes sins done in error from those done 'with a high hand' (defiantly, Numbers 15:30–31). The chatat addresses the former. Christ's intercession from the cross covers both: 'Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do' (Luke 23:34); his atoning work covers the full range of human failure.
2: A direct type: 'Speak to the people of Israel, saying, If anyone sins unintentionally [bishgagah] in any of YHWH's commandments about things not to be done, and does any one of them...' The sin offering is specifically for unintentional violations — sins of ignorance, weakness, and error. Peter frames the crucifixion itself as an act done in ignorance: 'you acted in ignorance, as did also your rulers' (Acts 3:17). Christ's prayer from the cross applies the chatat logic directly: the unknowing transgressor is covered by the sacrificial provision.
3: A direct type: 'If it is the anointed priest who sins, thus bringing guilt on the people, then he shall offer for the sin that he has committed a bull from the herd without blemish to YHWH for a sin offering.' The anointed priest's sin offering is the most costly — a whole bull. The irony is profound: the one appointed to bring others near to God must first bring his own sin offering. Hebrews presses this contrast: 'For it was indeed fitting that we should have such a high priest, holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners... He has no need, like those high priests, to offer sacrifices daily, first for his own sins and then for those of the people' (Hebrews 7:26–27). The Levitical priest's self-atonement requirement is the very deficiency Christ removes.
4: A direct type: 'He shall bring the bull to the entrance of the tent of meeting before YHWH and lay his hand on the head of the bull and kill the bull before YHWH.' Hand-laying appears even in the priest's own sin offering — the priest identifies with the bull just as any layman would. The sacrificial system makes no exemption for the one performing it; everyone who approaches must come through identification with the substitute. Christ 'learned obedience through what he suffered' (Hebrews 5:8) — he approached through suffering, not exemption from it.
5: A direct type: 'And the anointed priest shall take some of the blood of the bull and bring it into the tent of meeting.' The sin offering's blood is brought inside the tent — unlike the burnt and peace offerings whose blood is thrown against the outer altar. The penetration of blood into the sanctuary is the key typological act. Hebrews 9:11–12: 'But when Christ appeared as a high priest of the good things that have come... he entered once for all into the holy places, not by means of the blood of goats and calves but by means of his own blood, thus securing an eternal redemption.'
6: A direct type: 'And the priest shall dip his finger in the blood and sprinkle part of the blood seven times before YHWH in front of the veil of the sanctuary.' Seven sprinklings before the veil — the veil that separated the holy place from the most holy. Seven = completeness; the blood of the sin offering is completely applied to the boundary between holy and most holy. At Christ's death, 'the curtain of the temple was torn in two, from top to bottom' (Matthew 27:51) — the veil is not sprinkled but abolished. What the chatat blood reached toward, Christ's blood passed through entirely.
7: A shadow: 'And the priest shall put some of the blood on the horns of the altar of fragrant incense before YHWH that is in the tent of meeting, and all the rest of the blood of the bull he shall pour out at the base of the altar of burnt offering that is at the entrance of the tent of meeting.' Blood applied to both altars — the incense altar inside the tent and the burnt-offering altar outside. Christ's atonement reaches both: the inner sanctuary of prayer and the outer altar of sacrifice are both covered by his blood.
8: A shadow: 'And all the fat of the bull of the sin offering he shall remove from it, the fat covering the entrails and all the fat that is on the entrails.' The fat portions of the sin offering follow the same rule as the peace offering — given to YHWH. Even in the sin offering, the best is given to God: the sin-covering sacrifice does not merely manage sin but offers God what is richest.
9: A shadow: 'And the two kidneys with the fat that is on them at the loins, and the long lobe of the liver that he shall remove with the kidneys.' The inner organs — the seat of hidden life — given to God even in the sin offering. Christ's inner surrender to the Father in Gethsemane ('not my will, but yours, be done,' Luke 22:42) is the ultimate inner-organ offering: the will, the deepest kernel of the self, placed on the altar.
10: A shadow: 'Just as these are taken from the ox of the sacrifice of the peace offerings; and the priest shall burn them on the altar of burnt offering.' The sin offering's fat portions match the peace offering's — the same God who is reconciled in the peace offering receives the best in the sin offering. The atonement of sin and the establishment of peace are both given to the same God who owns all fat.
11: A direct type: 'But the skin of the bull and all its flesh, with its head, its legs, its entrails, and its dung — all the rest of the bull — he shall carry outside the camp to a clean place, to the ash heap, and shall burn it up on a fire of wood.' The sin offering bull is burned outside the camp — not on the altar, but at the ash heap. This is the key geographical type. Hebrews 13:11: 'For the bodies of those animals whose blood is brought into the holy places by the high priest as a sacrifice for sin are burned outside the camp.'
12: A direct type: 'He shall carry the bull outside the camp and burn it up. Where the ash heap is, there it shall be burned.' Hebrews 13:12–13 makes the explicit typological application: 'So Jesus also suffered outside the gate in order to sanctify the people through his own blood. Therefore let us go to him outside the camp and bear the reproach he endured.' The site of the sin offering's disposal — outside the gate, outside the camp, at the ash heap — is the exact type of the cross at Golgotha outside Jerusalem. The burning outside the camp tells Israel: atonement happens not inside the holy place but at the place of shame and disposal.
13: A direct type: 'If the whole congregation of Israel sins unintentionally and the thing is hidden from the eyes of the assembly, and they do any one of the things that by YHWH's commandments ought not to be done, and they realize their guilt...' The communal sin offering addresses the entire nation sinning as one. Christ's death is the communal atonement: 'it is better for you that one man should die for the people, not that the whole nation should perish' (John 11:50) — Caiaphas spoke better than he knew; the high priest's inadvertent prophecy echoes the communal chatat logic.
14: A shadow: 'When the sin which they have committed becomes known, the assembly shall offer a bull from the herd for a sin offering and bring it in front of the tent of meeting.' The communal sin offering requires the same grade of animal as the priestly sin offering — a bull. Corporate sin is as weighty as priestly sin. Christ's atonement bears the full weight of corporate human guilt: 'He is the propitiation for our sins, and not for ours only but also for the sins of the whole world' (1 John 2:2).
15: A direct type: 'The elders of the congregation shall lay their hands on the head of the bull before YHWH, and the bull shall be killed before YHWH.' The elders represent the entire congregation in hand-laying — the whole community's guilt is transferred via representative identification. Christ's death is represented by his disciples, apostles, and ultimately all who believe: 'all of us like sheep have gone astray... and YHWH laid on him the iniquity of us all' (Isaiah 53:6).
16: A direct type: 'Then the anointed priest shall bring some of the blood of the bull into the tent of meeting.' Repeated from v5 — the blood goes inside the tent, into the sanctuary. Whether for priestly sin (v5) or congregational sin (v16), the same act: blood brought inside. Christ's blood, by a single act, covers all categories: 'he entered once for all into the holy places... by means of his own blood, thus securing an eternal redemption' (Hebrews 9:12).
17: A direct type: 'And the priest shall dip his finger in the blood and sprinkle it seven times before YHWH in front of the veil.' The sevenfold blood-sprinkling before the veil is repeated for the communal sin offering (cf. v6 for the priestly sin offering). The repetition of the identical act emphasizes that the same barrier — the veil — separates God from humanity whether the sin is individual or communal. The veil torn at the crucifixion is not torn for one person but for all: 'the curtain of the temple was torn in two, from top to bottom' (Matthew 27:51) — from God's side, removing the barrier for all.
18: A shadow: 'And he shall put some of the blood on the horns of the altar that is in the tent of meeting before YHWH, and the rest of the blood he shall pour out at the base of the altar of burnt offering at the entrance of the tent of meeting.' Identical to v7 — blood on both altars. The repetition of the blood-rite for each category of sin (priest, congregation, leader, individual) drills into Israel: atonement requires the same act regardless of who sins. Christ's sacrifice is unrepeatable because he covered all categories in one act: 'by a single offering he has perfected for all time those who are being sanctified' (Hebrews 10:14).
19: A shadow: 'And all its fat he shall take from it and burn on the altar.' The fat to God — even in the communal sin offering, what is best goes to YHWH. Atonement is not merely housekeeping; it involves giving God the best in the midst of addressing the worst.
20: A direct type: 'Thus shall he do with the bull. As he did with the bull of the sin offering, so shall he do with this. And the priest shall make atonement for them, and they shall be forgiven.' The result: venislach lahem — 'and they shall be forgiven.' The sin offering consistently produces forgiveness (vv20, 26, 31, 35). Forgiveness is not God's reluctant concession but the designed outcome of the sacrifice. In Christ, this forgiveness is total and permanent: 'as far as the east is from the west, so far does he remove our transgressions from us' (Psalms 103:12); 'I will remember their sins no more' (Hebrews 10:17).
21: A direct type: 'And he shall carry the bull outside the camp and burn it up as he burned the first bull; it is the sin offering for the assembly.' The communal sin offering also burns outside the camp — the same as the priestly sin offering (v12). The repetition nails the outside-the-camp theme for the third time across Leviticus 4:11–12 and Leviticus 4:21. By the time Hebrews 13:11–13 cites it, Israel has been prepared through repeated exposure to this spatial type: atonement happens outside the holy zone, at the place of shame.
22: A shadow: 'When a leader sins, doing unintentionally any one of all the things that by the commandments of YHWH his God ought not to be done, and realizes his guilt...' The nasi (leader/ruler) requires a sin offering for his own unintentional sin. Leaders are not exempt — they are subject to the same need for atonement. The religious leaders who condemned Jesus did so thinking they were serving God; the sin offering for a leader's unwitting sin covers exactly this scenario (Acts 3:17).
23: A shadow: 'Or the sin which he has committed is made known to him, he shall bring as his offering a goat, a male without defect.' The leader's offering steps down from a bull to a male goat — the graduated scale of sin offerings matches the social hierarchy: priest (bull), congregation (bull), leader (male goat), individual (female goat or lamb). The gradation does not mean some sins are more forgivable but that each station carries its corresponding weight of responsibility.
24: A direct type: 'And he shall lay his hand on the head of the goat and kill it in the place where they kill the burnt offering before YHWH; it is a sin offering.' Hand-laying for the leader — the same act as for every other level. The sin offering's identification-requirement applies regardless of rank. Christ, who had no rank below him, took the place of identification with the lowest: 'he humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross' (Philippians 2:8).
25: A shadow: 'Then the priest shall take some of the blood of the sin offering with his finger and put it on the horns of the altar of burnt offering and pour out the rest of its blood at the base of the altar of burnt offering.' The leader's sin offering blood stays at the outer altar — unlike the priestly and congregational sin offerings whose blood enters the tent. The spatial gradient of blood application (inside the veil, outer altar) maps the graduated access to God's presence. Christ's blood, brought into the heavenly sanctuary itself, provides the highest access for all.
26: A direct type: 'And all its fat he shall burn on the altar, like the fat of the sacrifice of peace offerings. So the priest shall make atonement for him for his sin, and he shall be forgiven.' Venislach lo — 'and he shall be forgiven.' The sin offering's result is always forgiveness — the leader is forgiven exactly as the priest (v20), as the congregation (v20), as the individual will be (vv31, 35). The forgiveness is identical regardless of level. Christ's 'once for all' (Hebrews 10:10) forgiveness is equally distributed: 'there is no distinction: for all have sinned... and are justified by his grace as a gift' (Romans 3:22–24).
27: A direct type: 'If anyone of the common people sins unintentionally in doing any one of the things that by YHWH's commandments ought not to be done, and realizes his guilt...' The system culminates with the individual — any member of the common people. The sin offering was not only for priests and rulers but for every Israelite. Christ's atonement is personal: 'The Son of God loved me and gave himself for me' (Galatians 2:20). The individual in Leviticus 4:27 is the prototype of every believer for whom Christ died specifically.
28: A shadow: 'Or the sin that he has committed is made known to him, he shall bring for his offering a female goat without blemish [temimah], a female, for his sin which he has committed.' The individual sin offering is female — the lowest level in the graduated system. The consistent requirement of temimah (unblemished) at every level, including the least costly offering, maintains the principle: sinlessness in the substitute is non-negotiable regardless of economic level. No imperfect sacrifice can cover even one person's sin.
29: A direct type: 'And he shall lay his hand on the head of the sin offering and kill the sin offering in the place of burnt offering.' Hand-laying at the individual level — the last of four repetitions across chapter 4 (vv4, 15, 24, 29). The fourfold repetition across all social levels makes identification/substitution the unquestionable core of the entire sin offering system. Christ bore the hand-press of all humanity: 'He himself bore our sins in his body on the tree' (1 Peter 2:24).
30: A shadow: 'And the priest shall take some of its blood with his finger and put it on the horns of the altar of burnt offering and pour out all the rest of its blood at the base of the altar.' The individual's sin offering blood also remains at the outer altar — the same position as the leader's (v25). The spatial coding reflects that even the individual who cannot afford a bull receives the same effective atonement: 'For the Lord your God... is not partial and takes no bribe' (Deuteronomy 10:17).
31: A direct type: 'And all its fat he shall remove, as the fat is removed from the peace offerings, and the priest shall burn it on the altar for a pleasing aroma to YHWH. And the priest shall make atonement for him, and he shall be forgiven.' The third 'and he shall be forgiven' (venislach lo: vv20, 26, 31). The forgiveness formula is identical whether for a bull offered by the high priest or a female goat offered by the poorest Israelite. Christ's atonement produces the same forgiveness for all: 'in him we have redemption through his blood, the forgiveness of our trespasses, according to the riches of his grace' (Ephesians 1:7).
32: A shadow: 'If he brings a lamb as his offering for a sin offering, he shall bring a female without blemish.' The alternative individual sin offering — a female lamb instead of a female goat. The lamb option is the humblest form, requiring the same temimah (unblemished). This lamb-without-blemish, the individual's most basic provision, is the distant OT form of what John the Baptist sees: 'the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world' (John 1:29).
33: A direct type: 'And he shall lay his hand on the head of the sin offering and kill it for a sin offering in the place where they kill the burnt offering.' Even the lamb requires hand-laying — the final occurrence in chapter 4 of this central act. The sequence (bull/bull/goat/goat/lamb) ends with the lamb, the smallest and humblest. The NT sin offering will be a lamb — 'the Lamb who was slain from the foundation of the world' (Revelation 13:8).
34: A direct type: 'Then the priest shall take some of the blood of the sin offering with his finger and put it on the horns of the altar of burnt offering, and pour out all the rest of its blood at the base of the altar.' The blood of the individual's lamb is poured at the base of the altar — poured out, not just applied. Matthew 26:28: 'this is my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins.' The lamb's blood poured at the altar base is the type of Christ's blood poured out at the cross.
35: A direct type: 'And all its fat he shall remove as the fat of the lamb is removed from the sacrifice of peace offerings, and the priest shall burn it on the altar, on top of YHWH's food offerings. And the priest shall make atonement for him for the sin which he has committed, and he shall be forgiven.' The fourth and final 'and he shall be forgiven' (venislach lo: vv20, 26, 31, 35) closes the sin offering chapter. The fourfold repetition (priest, congregation, leader, individual) covers every level of the community. Christ's one offering covers all four levels simultaneously and permanently: 'by a single offering he has perfected for all time those who are being sanctified' (Hebrews 10:14). The graduated Levitical system required a separate sacrifice for each level; Christ's sacrifice is the one act that achieves what the fourfold system only approximated.
